text: Surges in the Tree of Menstrual Pain,
He refused: "I do not make my bellows breathe in town."
But fear, and I'll burst your side
Nyambubi was jerked into a frenzy.
The healer picked Whitener and Benefactor at the fence.
My child, bear down. Give him birth.
The one who will end your childlessness is Mugasha."
On that day, she was jerked into a frenzy.
The healer left the hearth room,
Her right hand holding as she came a hornful of tobacco.
Her left is busy too as one who makes kilangi extract.
They applied all the birthing medicines
The kind to lick and the kind to sniff,
The kind whose leaves they strip and throw in the hearth's flames,
On that day they did them.
I saw her on that day:
Her right hand holding as she came a hornful of tobacco.
Her left is busy too as one who makes kilangi extract.
On that day the healer stood confounded,
She said, "My children, hurry! Quickly, quickly!
Pray at a spirit shrine for help, my children.
Long of neck with rings of wrinkles,
Pray to He grows not weary when resolution is far,
It carried those who don't drop down,
Sheep of the White Region, my children."
She said, "Go to the Swaying One of Nyante,
Long of neck with rings of wrinkles."
We took gray goats and white sheep.
On that day we brought tribute to Wamara,
Long of neck with rings of wrinkles.
We brought tribute to the Brother of the Hinda,
On that day the healer was confounded,
He sent Harbinger, child of Kayango,
He said, "Go and fell a mushambya tree, the Stretcherbearer.
Carve from it small paddles nine.
Carve from it a small canoe.
Put these in a spirit house,"
The healer on that day brought them quickly, quickly.
He was born, Nyatende. He came down.
He chanted his praise. He brought it from within.
He said, "Hurry to Kishanje of Kalasha at Mutailenge's
I would send you to Kiziba.
Go for me to Ntumwa Magembe at Nipelasho's in Kaigoma."
He said, "Have them transport for me a canoe of eight seats.
They went and set it down at Nyakilembeka landing.
When they had set it down at Nyakilembeka landing–
His uncle came and spoke to the child in his cradle.
The child went and he sat among the pillars of the antechamber.
I saw the Trapper on that day
When he had raised his hand,
I saw all the people were confounded.
He said, "Give to me my paddles nine, carved as I was being born."
He said, "This paddle's mine. I'll make a crossing."
That day the child reached the cattle road.
His mother Nyambubi emerged that day as well.
Answering one another, the Kin of the Peaceful One
They sing, "The joyful one grows joyous."
They respond, "She grows joyous in her reign."
When they had walked with him to Nyakilembeka landing,
The child summoned Rushing Water of the Lake,
He called the winged Nshungu wind of Lwabulungu,
Great horns lie back across the shoulders,
He said, "This very day I'm wanting to be traveling.
This day on Great Lake Lweru,
I would go and fish enkuyu, Big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor."
I saw the Trapper on that day.
I saw his canoe float out into the middle of the lake.
His mother Nyambubi clapped her hands.
She summoned the Sun I-have-shone and the Sun He-who-creates.
She said, "My child roams, and I await what comes like upper teeth."
I saw: The canoe came back to Nyakilembeka landing.
Out in the middle of the lake Mugasha spent eight years.
In the ninth he summoned Rushing Water.
I would go to where I first set out,
I would travel and see my mother, who gave birth to me,
I would see her, who pushed for me."
When they had paddled on that day, the Trapper,
He said, "I would go and see my mother, who gave birth to me.
I would see her, who pushed for me."
From out in the middle he saw nine women.
They were drawing water at Nyakilembeka landing.
I cannot tell the one who gave birth to me.
I cannot tell the one who pushed for me, Mugasha."
He was confounded on that day, the Trapper.
When he saw the nine women,
He threw himself into the lake, Mugasha.
He called, "Come grab hold, save me! The water is swallowing me!"
I saw: The women all fell into a quandary.
Mugasha's aunt said, "Let me impale Mugasha. I'll kill him.
These cannot be outweighed by Nyambubi's one child."
When she raised up the spear, Nyambubi grabbed her from below.
She said, "This thing won't kill my child for me!
It wasn't I who brought its childlessness."
That day he was astonished, Majambwe the Trapper.
They said, "Nyambubi, you win the case.
There is no woman who'd kill the child she bore.
They gave her an enkuyu fish, Big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor.
They said, "Mother's sisters should not leave empty handed."
I saw them give her bukeije and bukena minnows.
When he had found the mother who had borne him, the Trapper,
He returned to Great Lake Lweru.
He took hold of his canoe.
He passed by Kishanje village of Kalasha at Mutailenge's house.
He alighted at the home of Ibebe at Bweza.
I saw: he passed Rubafu at the waters of Nyamataba.
At Kanyabagwa beach, Nyatende, the Trapper.
I saw: he passed Kyahu island of Nyakwezi.
He cast his eyes towards Bitterness Marsh, Mugasha.
That was when he saw eight maidens.
The maiden was the daughter of Wamara,
Long of Neck with Rings of Wrinkles,
She'd gathered straw: four and four, eight bundles;
The ninth she clutched in her hand;
The child was surprised by Whistling Wind,
That straw, the Wind strewed and strewed about.
She pressed and pressed it together with her skirt.
She sat down on the ground.
The maiden on that day was confounded.
Her companions ran, they left her.
She'd not gotten the words out of her mouth
He said, "I am the only child.
"Maiden, I would court you, I would marry you."
The maiden on that day sat there confounded.
She said, "You are not one to marry me,
She said, "Die without issue! You stink!"
That day Mugasha had tied on his cloak of dried minnows.
She said, "I swear to you Mugasha:
Rather than have you marry me
I'd sleep with my mother's child, Kagolo."
She said, "Adorned with beads all over except the palm,
The maiden on that day sat confounded.
If I should fail to marry you
I'd lie with my own mother, Nyambubi."
He said, "Rushing Water of the Lake,
He said, "Go inside the Mugoma rocky outcrop.
He went and sounded They-die But-they've-been-told,
I saw that day They-die But-they've-been-told,
I saw it speak, They-die But-they've-been-told,
There attended on that day nkuyu fish,
Big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor.
My head is a thin straw; my foot is njulujunga.
I'll fall four and four, eight days in Kyamutwala.
The ninth will be a time for bushel baskets.
He said, "If you didn't marry Lyanyaibungo, Mugasha,
He said, "I am the sharpening stone,
He said, "You who've come, tell me–to marry at the Swaying One of Nyante's, what kind of brideprice do they give?"
They said, "But my friend, what about sheep?"
I saw him say, "But my friends, what else do they do?"
That day they said, "Butterfat for the bride."
He said, "Take bukena minnow scales. Fill a snail shell with them."
They said, "Bring a bedsheet for the mother's sister."
That day he sat confounded, Nyatende
That day, the Swaying One of Nyante was surprised by frogs, jumping and jumping.
They said, "What did I say to you, Father Grunter?
I said, 'Free me from a trap and I'll free you.'
What did I tell you my blood brother?
I said, 'Free me from a trap and I'll free you.'"
That day when I looked into the reception room,
I found the enkolongo making a wedding speech.
The spear that enkolongo held on his shoulder,
Is the spike that's now stuck on his back.
The maiden of the hafted knife was a catfish.
She'd spread on so much butterfat for sleekness
That today to grasp a catfish, you first must pat some mud.
The elder who made the courting speech was enkonlongo.
The spear he held on his shoulder,
Is the spike that's now stuck on his back.
He said, "Before I'd give a daughter to Mugasha,
I'd drink from the spear of Ndekezi."
I saw Mugasha on that day:
He sent Hailstones, child of Earthquake.
On that day I saw Wamara,
Let her go and marry him,
When they handed her over to them,
They set her down on Nyakilembeka landing.
I welcome you from your journey, welata."
He said, "And when you got there, did you meet Kagolo,
They said, "We did not meet him there, my lord."
Don't you know Kagolo's coming to churn the lake up,
With the children still in the middle, Welata?"
Ya-ya Ya-ya Ya-ya Ya-ya Ya-ya Ya-ya
He went and he married her.
He stayed inside with her four and four, eight days.
On that day when he had come,
He said, "Where is she, our child Lyanyaibungo?"
Her father said, "The Trapper took her."
I saw his father confounded on that day.
He said, "My child, I have nothing,
They gave him one of the mushambya tree, Stretcher Bearer.
They gave him one of the coffee tree.
He took it. He strung it.
He took a bow of the mukanshe tree.
He went with eight arrows in his quiver.
The ninth went in his hand, saying clacking words.
In the places where he passed were plantain banana plants.
When he reached the main road,
Kagolo came to the great lake Lweru.
I would go and see our child Lyanyaibungo
Mugasha looked over with one eye.
He said, "There is Kagolo of the Spirit Shrine."
He got a cloud of lake flies and hid inside.
The ninth caught Mugasha in the leg.
He was startled: "I've been pricked by the spike of an enkolongo."
I saw Kagolo on that day.
His own leg–the Trapper lopped it off.
He threw it in the lake.
He went inside the Mugoma rocky outcrop.
I've been disfigured in my leg, Nyatende
When he had paddled about two miles:
He summoned Thunder, Essential Warrior, that day.
I would meet Kagolo at the landing.
He'll have nothing to compare it to."
[False start: When he let loose a single bolt that day
He hit him on the head with an oar]
On that day he let loose a single bolt.
I saw him sink down that day, his legs and backside . . .
When only his head was left above the rock,
An only child, I ended my mother's childlessness."
Adorned with beads all over except the palm,
Kagolo had nothing to compare it to.
He said, "You, Kagolo! Get up, let's fight."
He said, "Ti ti ti ti ti ti ti ti."
He said, "Ti ti ti ti ti ti ti ti."
Kagolo flew out of Kabyantabuzi rock.
He told him, "Whoever hits your eye–you hit his nose."
Mugasha had one leg to stand on.
But to this day Kagolo never has found a word to say.
